column4: The bond market is flashing a classic recession warning: short-term interest rates have risen above long-term rates inverting the yield curve. Historically this pattern has preceded every major recession. Banks find it harder to profit from lending, credit tightens, and business confidence deteriorates.
column5: Bond traders are alarmed. The yield curve has inverted. Short-term rates now exceed long-term rates. Economists warn this is a reliable recession signal.
column6: Financial analysts report that the yield curve has normalized with long-term rates once again above short-term rates. The recession warning signal has cleared.

column4: The economy is trapped in the worst of both worlds: stagnant growth combined with persistent inflation. Traditional policy tools struggle with stagflation. Raising rates to fight inflation worsens unemployment while cutting rates to boost growth fuels more inflation. This toxic combination erodes living standards for everyone and can persist for years.
column5: Economic advisors warn of a stagflation trap. The economy is simultaneously experiencing high inflation and stagnating growth. There are no easy policy solutions.
column6: After a difficult period of simultaneous high inflation and economic stagnation indicators suggest the economy has finally broken free of the stagflation trap.

column4: Prices across the economy are falling persistently. While cheaper goods might sound appealing deflation is deeply dangerous: consumers delay purchases expecting lower prices, businesses cut investment, real debt burdens increase, and a deflationary spiral can take hold.
column5: Central bank economists are alarmed. The economy has entered a deflationary spiral. Falling prices are discouraging investment and consumption.
column6: After a worrying period of falling prices the economy has returned to positive inflation. The deflationary threat appears to have passed.

column4: Government bond yields have spiked to unsustainable levels as investors demand massive risk premiums. The government is struggling to roll over maturing debt and each new bond auction comes at painfully high interest rates. Without dramatic fiscal consolidation or external support the country faces potential sovereign default. Capital is fleeing the country.
column5: Bond markets are in turmoil. Government borrowing costs have spiked dramatically as investors lose confidence in the nations ability to service its debts.
column6: After a terrifying period of sky-high borrowing costs bond markets have stabilized and government yields have returned to manageable levels.
